channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA's Psyche mission delivers Mars flyby data, time-lapse video - Phys.org
item.description:
1. NASA's Psyche mission delivers Mars flyby data, time-lapse video  Phys.org
  2. NASA’s Psyche Mission Delivers Mars Flyby Data, Time-lapse Video  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. Image: The growing crescent of Mars as NASA's psyche mission approaches  Phys.org
  4. NASA Shares Beautiful Timelapse Of The Psyche Spacecraft's View Over Mars  Engadget
  5. Watch this incredible time-lapse of the NASA Psyche mission’s Mars flyby.  The Verge

item.title: 'It's absolutely exciting!': Astronomers discover an atmosphere around an Earth-like planet in the habitable zone for first time ever - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'It's absolutely exciting!': Astronomers discover an atmosphere around an Earth-like planet in the habitable zone for first time ever  Live Science
  2. Astronomers Find an Atmosphere on a Nearby Earthlike Planet  The New York Times
  3. Earth-like exoplanet found to have an atmosphere  The Guardian
  4. Scientists Discover a Planet With the Most Earth-like Conditions Yet  Time Magazine
  5. First atmosphere found on Earth-like planet in habitable zone of distant star  BBC

item.title: Meteorite that hit New Jersey home may unlock clues to life’s origins - FOX 8 News
item.description:
1. Meteorite that hit New Jersey home may unlock clues to life’s origins  FOX 8 News
  2. Alien World Chemistry Found Inside Meteorite That Struck New Jersey home  SETI
  3. Meteorite that crashed into New Jersey home contains building blocks of life, astronomers say  ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos
  4. Meteorite that crashed into New Jersey home contains "alien world chemistry," scientists say  CBS News
  5. Meteorite that crashed into a New Jersey home contains ‘extraterrestrial’ amino acids  CNN

item.description:
1. Why a Single Sun‑Reflecting Satellite Has Scientists Sounding the Alarm  Yahoo

  4. A US tech start-up wants to turn night into day. The question is why?  ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation
  5. FCC Approves First Launch for Space Reflector Constellation  Universe Today

item.title: We assume life needs a sun, but a team at Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich calculated in 2026 that moons orbiting starless rogue planets could sustain liquid oceans for up to 4.3 billion years — roughly as long as Earth has existed — without a single ra - Space Daily
item.description: We assume life needs a sun, but a team at Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich calculated in 2026 that moons orbiting starless rogue planets could sustain liquid oceans for up to 4.3 billion years — roughly as long as Earth has existed — without a single ra  Space Daily

item.title: We’re Underestimating the Power of ‘One-in-a-Thousand-Year’ Solar Storms, NASA Warns - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. We’re Underestimating the Power of ‘One-in-a-Thousand-Year’ Solar Storms, NASA Warns  Gizmodo
  2. Are we underestimating the threat of solar storms? A 'once-in-a-thousand-year' disaster is worth considering, scientists say  Space
  3. Regression to the mean can explain saturation of geomagnetic storms  Nature
  4. New NASA Study Says Possibly No Limit to Solar Storm Effects  NASA Science (.gov)
  5. Power grids face unexpected slow-burn threat from solar storms  The Hindu

item.title: In 1970, Venera 7 appeared to fall silent when it hit Venus, but engineers later found 23 minutes of faint radio data buried in the recording — the first transmission ever received from the surface of another planet - Space Daily
item.description: In 1970, Venera 7 appeared to fall silent when it hit Venus, but engineers later found 23 minutes of faint radio data buried in the recording — the first transmission ever received from the surface of another planet  Space Daily

item.title: Astronauts returning from long stays in orbit say the smell of rain is almost too much to bear and a familiar street feels like something they are piloting rather than driving - Space Daily
item.description: Astronauts returning from long stays in orbit say the smell of rain is almost too much to bear and a familiar street feels like something they are piloting rather than driving  Space Daily

item.title: 10 places in the US and Canada to see August's partial solar eclipse and Perseid meteor shower in 1 trip - Space
item.description:
1. 10 places in the US and Canada to see August's partial solar eclipse and Perseid meteor shower in 1 trip  Space

  3. It’s Not Too Late to Book a Trip to See the August 2026 Eclipse—Here’s How You Can Do It  AFAR
  4. A solar eclipse is coming to Europe this summer  The Washington Post
  5. SiteMinder: Total solar eclipse drives up to 76% growth in bookings for Spain’s hotels  Hospitality Net

item.title: When Spirit’s front wheel failed on Mars, engineers drove the rover backwards and let the dead wheel scrape through the soil — accidentally uncovering bright silica that pointed to ancient hot springs or steam vents - Space Daily
item.description: When Spirit’s front wheel failed on Mars, engineers drove the rover backwards and let the dead wheel scrape through the soil — accidentally uncovering bright silica that pointed to ancient hot springs or steam vents  Space Daily

  4. JWST reveals how a supermassive black hole keeps feeding itself  Yahoo

  4. After record heat, could the Atlantic make Britain's weather even more extreme?  BBC

item.title: Scientists used children’s ‘silly sprinklers’ to solve a physics problem Richard Feynman could not; the r - The Times of India
item.description:
1. Scientists used children’s ‘silly sprinklers’ to solve a physics problem Richard Feynman could not; the r  The Times of India
  2. Physicists Have Argued Over This Problem Since 1883. It May Now Be Solved.  The New York Times
  3. “Silly sprinklers” help scientists finally solve Feynman’s famous sprinkler mystery  ScienceDaily
  4. Feynman Sprinkler Problem Solved: Momentum Flux, Not Fluid Swirl, Spins Both Ways  Tech Times
  5. Mines researcher helps put “silly sprinklers” in reverse to unravel decades-old physics puzzle  Mines Newsroom

item.title: Plants that make real dairy protein? Scientists discover an unexpected shortcut - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Plants that make real dairy protein? Scientists discover an unexpected shortcut  Phys.org
  2. Bovine Casein Grown in Plant Seeds Reaches 1.26% Protein Yield in Miruku-Linked Study  Cultivated X

  4. Can Plants Make Dairy Protein Without Cows? New Study Comes Close  StudyFinds

item.title: Astronauts who have seen the whole Earth at once describe the overview effect — every war and border and argument hanging in silence — and afterwards traffic and small talk can feel strangely far away, like the world seen through glass - Space Daily
item.description: Astronauts who have seen the whole Earth at once describe the overview effect — every war and border and argument hanging in silence — and afterwards traffic and small talk can feel strangely far away, like the world seen through glass  Space Daily

item.description:
1. Scientists observe ‘negative time’ for the first time in a quantum experiment  The Brighter Side of News
  2. Quantum time travel: The experiment to ‘send a particle into the past’  New Scientist
  3. Time stops along with chaos in the quantum Universe  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Pit found by Quebecer confirmed to be a 390-million-years-old meteorite impact crater - CTV News
item.description:
1. Pit found by Quebecer confirmed to be a 390-million-years-old meteorite impact crater  CTV News
  2. Giant pit discovered by hiker on Google Maps confirmed to be 390 million-year-old meteor impact crater  Live Science
  3. He saw a pit on Google Maps. It turned out to be a 390-million-year-old meteor crater  CBC
  4. More than one way to make an impact  The Planetary Society
  5. Quebecer discovers 390-million-year-old crater  Yahoo News Canada

item.title: A century-old stellar mystery may finally have an explanation - Phys.org
item.description: A century-old stellar mystery may finally have an explanation  Phys.org

item.title: Cassini watched bright “islands” appear, disappear and reappear in Titan’s methane seas — and laboratory experiments suggest they may be fields of nitrogen bubbles fizzing out of solution as the alien lakes warm or mix - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Cassini watched bright “islands” appear, disappear and reappear in Titan’s methane seas — and laboratory experiments suggest they may be fields of nitrogen bubbles fizzing out of solution as the alien lakes warm or mix  Space Daily
  2. Saturn's moon Titan runs the same weather cycle as Earth — clouds, rain, rivers and seas — except the liquid doing all the work is methane, and the bedrock underfoot is water frozen at nearly minus 180 degrees, harder than most stone on Earth  Space Daily
  3. Saturn’s Largest Moon May Hold the Resources for a Space Colony  SciTechDaily
  4. Titan could be humanity’s next deep space destination  A News
  5. Even at noon Titan sits in permanent dusk: distance and thick haze together leave barely a thousandth of Earth's sunlight at the surface — about as dim as Earth ten minutes after sunset, a golden gloom that never lifts  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists told them, 'No, it's too dangerous,' but they did it anyway: Inside Japan's super-close asteroid flyby - Space
item.description:
1. Scientists told them, 'No, it's too dangerous,' but they did it anyway: Inside Japan's super-close asteroid flyby  Space
  2. Jersey Skies: Two asteroid flybys  Jersey's Best
  3. Nasa knows how to deflect an asteroid. Can Japan’s Hayabusa2 pull it off?  South China Morning Post
  4. Japan’s upper-close asteroid flyby: Why scientists said it was too dangerous  The News International
  5. Hayabusa2’s Next Target Is A Tiny 11 Meter Asteroid  Hackaday

item.title: Across Titan’s equator, winds have built dunes around 100 metres high and hundreds of kilometres long — but the grains are water ice coated in hydrocarbons that fell out of the moon’s orange atmosphere - Space Daily
item.description: Across Titan’s equator, winds have built dunes around 100 metres high and hundreds of kilometres long — but the grains are water ice coated in hydrocarbons that fell out of the moon’s orange atmosphere  Space Daily

item.title: Diagnosed with Stage 4 cancer, an owl researcher kept working till the end - The Washington Post
item.description: Diagnosed with Stage 4 cancer, an owl researcher kept working till the end  The Washington Post

item.title: The Sahara turns green roughly every 20,000 years, and the last time it did, hippos and crocodiles lived in lakes where there is now only sand - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Sahara turns green roughly every 20,000 years, and the last time it did, hippos and crocodiles lived in lakes where there is now only sand  Space Daily
  2. We tend to think of the Sahara as the world's great permanent desert, but researchers have found it was a green savanna with hippos, giraffes, and rivers as recently as 6,000 years ago — and that it collapsed into desert within one to two centuries, a tipping po  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: They Don’t Fight Crime, but They Do Eat Bugs: The Bats of N.Y.C. - The New York Times
item.description: They Don’t Fight Crime, but They Do Eat Bugs: The Bats of N.Y.C.  The New York Times

item.description:
1. 1.7K-Pound Great White Cruises by Outer Banks  Newser
  2. Largest tagged male great white shark in Atlantic spotted near Outer Banks  13newsnow.com
  3. Massive great white shark spotted off NC coast. See where its headed  USA Today
  4. Largest male great white shark ever detected on Atlantic coast emerges near top vacation spot  Yahoo
  5. Atlantic’s largest male great white Contender likely heading toward this popular summer getaway  FOX Weather

item.title: In 1980, Voyager 1 flew close enough to Titan to reveal its thick nitrogen atmosphere — but the gravity-assist geometry bent the spacecraft permanently out of the planetary plane, trading Uranus and Neptune for one close look at Saturn’s largest moon - Space Daily
item.description: In 1980, Voyager 1 flew close enough to Titan to reveal its thick nitrogen atmosphere — but the gravity-assist geometry bent the spacecraft permanently out of the planetary plane, trading Uranus and Neptune for one close look at Saturn’s largest moon  Space Daily

item.title: Cells from your mother likely infiltrated your brain in the womb, and they could survive for decades, study reveals - Live Science
item.description: Cells from your mother likely infiltrated your brain in the womb, and they could survive for decades, study reveals  Live Science

item.title: China plans to hit an asteroid with a Mach 26 projectile in planet defence test - South China Morning Post
item.description:
1. China plans to hit an asteroid with a Mach 26 projectile in planet defence test  South China Morning Post
  2. China plans bold asteroid collision test to outpace NASA's DART mission  Interesting Engineering
  3. China plans to hit a 30-meter asteroid at more than 9 kilometers per second and will use two spacecraft to find out if it can change the rock's trajectory during its passage about 7 million kilometers from Earth.  CPG Click Oil and Gas
  4. China is building an asteroid monitoring network that will use telescopes and satellites for planetary defence: Here’s how it will work  The Times of India
  5. China plans to surpass NASA’s DART mission by smacking an asteroid at 26 times the speed of sound  starlust.org

item.title: Geologists have taught for decades that rivers needed plants to form their distinctive S-shaped meanders, but Stanford University researchers published a study in August 2025 showing that rivers were carving those curves long before vegetation existed on la - Space Daily
item.description: Geologists have taught for decades that rivers needed plants to form their distinctive S-shaped meanders, but Stanford University researchers published a study in August 2025 showing that rivers were carving those curves long before vegetation existed on la  Space Daily

item.title: When elephants play, they rapidly copy one another’s trunk and head movements — a form of bodily mimicry resembling the contagious smiles and laughter seen in humans - Space Daily
item.description: When elephants play, they rapidly copy one another’s trunk and head movements — a form of bodily mimicry resembling the contagious smiles and laughter seen in humans  Space Daily

item.title: Enceladus, a Saturn moon just 500 kilometres wide, fires geysers of water thousands of kilometres into space through cracks at its south pole — and that water carries the building blocks of life - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Enceladus, a Saturn moon just 500 kilometres wide, fires geysers of water thousands of kilometres into space through cracks at its south pole — and that water carries the building blocks of life  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s moon Enceladus is spraying a hidden ocean into space — and inside that spray is the same chemical fuel that feeds life in Earth’s deepest oceans  Space Daily

item.title: In 2004 a NASA probe caught a comet's tail on a sheet of aerogel, flew the grains 4.6 billion kilometres home, and dropped them into the Utah desert — the first solid pieces of a comet ever returned to Earth - Space Daily
item.description: In 2004 a NASA probe caught a comet's tail on a sheet of aerogel, flew the grains 4.6 billion kilometres home, and dropped them into the Utah desert — the first solid pieces of a comet ever returned to Earth  Space Daily

item.description:
1. This New Process Turns Plastic Trash Into Clean Fuel Instead of Pollution  Gizmodo
  2. New process turns mixed plastic waste directly into hydrogen fuel without sorting  Phys.org
  3. Chemical treatment slashes emissions of making hydrogen from plastic waste  Chemistry World
  4. Single-reactor process yields 90% pure hydrogen fuel from mixed plastic waste  Interesting Engineering

item.title: Mercury is shrinking — as its iron core slowly cools, the whole planet has contracted by up to seven kilometres, wrinkling its surface into cliffs hundreds of miles long - Space Daily
item.description: Mercury is shrinking — as its iron core slowly cools, the whole planet has contracted by up to seven kilometres, wrinkling its surface into cliffs hundreds of miles long  Space Daily

item.title: An AI searched nearly 100 million old Hubble images in two and a half days and found 1,300 cosmic oddities — more than 800 of them had never appeared in the scientific literature - Space Daily
item.description: An AI searched nearly 100 million old Hubble images in two and a half days and found 1,300 cosmic oddities — more than 800 of them had never appeared in the scientific literature  Space Daily

item.title: A primitive dwarf galaxy with almost none of the heavy elements found in the Milky Way is somehow producing iron and silicon-carbide dust — materials astronomers thought early galaxies would struggle to make - Space Daily
item.description: A primitive dwarf galaxy with almost none of the heavy elements found in the Milky Way is somehow producing iron and silicon-carbide dust — materials astronomers thought early galaxies would struggle to make  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists identify the rare meteorite that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago - ScienceDaily
item.description:
1. Scientists identify the rare meteorite that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago  ScienceDaily
  2. Dinosaurs May Have Been Wiped Out by ‘Oddball’ Space Rock  Sci.News
  3. Researchers identify class of “oddball” meteorite that killed the dinosaurs  The University of British Columbia
  4. Nickel Fingerprints Reveal What the Asteroid That Killed the Dinosaurs Was Made Of  ZME Science
  5. The asteroid that killed the dinosaurs was more odd than expected  The Brighter Side of News

item.description: DNA from 457 koalas suggests the species nearly went extinct 100,000 years ago, before humans reached Aus  The Times of India

item.title: Researchers extracted water directly from lunar soil in July 2025 and used sunlight to convert it into breathable oxygen and rocket fuel — cutting out any need to ship water from Earth, where a single gallon costs an estimated $83,000 to launch - Space Daily
item.description: Researchers extracted water directly from lunar soil in July 2025 and used sunlight to convert it into breathable oxygen and rocket fuel — cutting out any need to ship water from Earth, where a single gallon costs an estimated $83,000 to launch  Space Daily

item.title: New Black Hole Theory Solves a 50-Year-Old Problem - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. New Black Hole Theory Solves a 50-Year-Old Problem  SciTechDaily
  2. Optical fiber black hole model emits Hawking-like radiation, revealing a long-sought recoil  Yahoo
  3. Stephen Hawking's famous 'leaky' black hole theory gets much-needed update  Space
  4. 'Smaller than the tiniest scale in nature': Physicists made a black hole out of light and used it to test Stephen Hawking's elusive radiation theory  Live Science
  5. Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade  ScienceDaily

item.title: Voyager 1 has no engine running. The 38,000-mile-per-hour speed it has held since its Saturn flyby in 1980 is borrowed momentum from planets it grazed more than four decades ago, and that free ride carries it to one light-day from Earth this November - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 has no engine running. The 38,000-mile-per-hour speed it has held since its Saturn flyby in 1980 is borrowed momentum from planets it grazed more than four decades ago, and that free ride carries it to one light-day from Earth this November  Space Daily
  2. NASA has calculated, to the precise second, when Voyager 1 will be exactly one light-day from Earth: 2:16 a.m. on 18 November 2026, at a distance of 16,094,799,096 miles — a number that would have been science fiction when the probe launched in 1977  Space Daily
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  5. NASA's Voyager 1 Only Has Two Active Science Instruments Left After Latest System Shutdown  Hypebeast

item.title: Spend A Day As A Sea Bunny And We’ll Tell You Which Ocean You Belong In - buzzfeed.com
item.description: Spend A Day As A Sea Bunny And We’ll Tell You Which Ocean You Belong In  buzzfeed.com

item.title: Scientists use relay synthesis to create key building blocks of reserve antibiotic to combat resistance - Phys.org
item.description: Scientists use relay synthesis to create key building blocks of reserve antibiotic to combat resistance  Phys.org

item.title: Physicists Just Turned a Black Hole Energy Theory Into Reality - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. Physicists Just Turned a Black Hole Energy Theory Into Reality  SciTechDaily
  2. Researchers Prove Black Hole Theory in a Laboratory Setting  Universe Today
  3. 🌀 Physicists extract energy from a 'black hole' in the lab  Techno-Science
  4. Recreating the 'Stealing Energy from a Black Hole' Thought Experiment on a Tabletop: How an Electronic Circuit Overturned the Principles of Wave Amplification  XenoSpectrum
  5. Scientists recreate black hole energy effect in lab  Azərtac
